perturbation.original_text: 4.1 For two (2) years following the Effective Time, QIWI covenants that at least one half of the total transactions processed by the Contact money transfer system during such two-year period shall be settled through PJSC Khanty-Mansiysk Bank Otkritie, provided that QIWI may terminate its obligation under this Clause 4.1 at any time, in which case it shall pay to Otkritie a fee equal to the demonstrated costs incurred by Otkritie for such period starting from 1 September 2014, until the date of termination of QIWI's obligations under this Clause 4.1 in connection with (i) the transfer of settlement bank function to PJSC Khanty-Mansiysk Bank Otkritie ; and (ii) supporting the settlement bank functionality for the Contact money transfer system, provided further that such fee shall in no event exceed RUB 50 000 000 (fifty million roubles).
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1 For one (1) year following the Effective Time, QIWI covenants that at least one half of the total transactions processed by the Contact money transfer system during such one-year period shall be settled through PJSC Khanty-Mansiysk Bank Otkritie, provided that QIWI may terminate its obligation under this Clause 4.1 at any time, in which case it shall pay to Otkritie a fee equal to the demonstrated costs incurred by Otkritie for such period starting from 1 September 2014, until the date of termination of QIWI's obligations under this Clause 4.1 in connection with (i) the transfer of settlement bank function to PJSC Khanty-Mansiysk Bank Otkritie ; and (ii) supporting the settlement bank functionality for the Contact money transfer system, provided further that such fee shall in no event exceed RUB 5 000 000 (five million roubles).
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the commitment period and the maximum termination fee may violate anti-tying regulations or fair competition laws, especially if Otkritie used its influence to secure this agreement. Furthermore, decreasing the maximum fee to an unreasonably low amount creates an unfair advantage.
perturbation.law_explanation: Reducing the commitment period to one year and decreasing the maximum termination fee to five million roubles could be seen as creating an anti-competitive tying arrangement that violates Section 1 of the Sherman Antitrust Act. If QIWI was coerced into using Otkritie's bank services at unfavorable terms and with a limited termination fee, it could be construed as a restraint of trade.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 15 / CHAPTER 1 / § 1 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 15 USC 1 : Trusts, etc., in restraint of trade illegal; penalty Text contains those laws in effect on June 14, 2025 From Title 15-COMMERCE AND TRADE CHAPTER 1-MONOPOLIES AND COMBINATIONS IN RESTRAINT OF TRADE Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous Amendments Effective Date Short Title Executive Documents §1. Trusts, etc., in restraint of trade illegal; penalty Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, is declared to be illegal. Every person who shall make any contract or engage in any combination or conspiracy hereby declared to be illegal shall be
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the commitment period from two years to one year and decreases the maximum termination fee from fifty million roubles to five million roubles. The LLM explanation suggests this could create an anti-competitive tying arrangement violating Section 1 of the Sherman Antitrust Act if QIWI was coerced into using Otkritie's bank services at unfavorable terms with a limited termination fee. Scraped Law Snippet 1, which cites 15 USC 1, states that "Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, is declared to be illegal." The reduction in commitment period and termination fee, combined with the possibility of coercion as described in the LLM explanation, could be viewed as creating a restraint of trade, thereby creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 8.1 A notice (including any approval, consent or other communication) in connection with this Agreement and the documents referred to in it or with any arbitration under this Agreement: (a) must be in writing in English; (b) for the avoidance of doubt, must not be sent by electronic mail; (c) must be delivered by hand, fax or courier using an internationally recognised courier company to the address specified below in relation to the Party to whom the notice is addressed, and marked for the attention of the person so specified, or to such other address or fax number or marked for the attention of such other person, as the relevant party may from time to time specify by notice given in accordance with this clause.
perturbation.changed_text: 8.1 A notice (including any approval, consent or other communication) in connection with this Agreement and the documents referred to in it or with any arbitration under this Agreement: (a) must be in writing in English; (b) and must be sent by electronic mail; (c) must be delivered to the address specified below in relation to the Party to whom the notice is addressed, and marked for the attention of the person so specified, or to such other address or electronic number or marked for the attention of such other person, as the relevant party may from time to time specify by notice given in accordance with this clause.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring notice *only* by email may not be legally sufficient, especially for critical communications like those related to arbitration. This is because some jurisdictions and legal frameworks require a method of delivery that provides proof of receipt, or allows for verification of delivery.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act (E-Sign Act)
perturbation.law_explanation: While the E-Sign Act generally validates electronic signatures and records, it doesn't automatically override specific requirements for notice in certain legal contexts.  If arbitration rules or applicable law require 'written notice' delivered in a manner that ensures proof of receipt, mandating exclusive email notice could invalidate those requirements. Section 7001 outlines requirements for electronic records to satisfy legal writing requirements. It implicitly requires methods to ensure notice is received and can be verified, which exclusive email use might not always guarantee, potentially contradicting the spirit and intent of legally sound notification.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: [106th Congress Public Law 229] [From the U.S. Government Printing Office] <DOC> [DOCID: f:publ229.106] [[Page 463]] ELECTRONIC SIGNATURES IN GLOBAL AND NATIONAL COMMERCE ACT [[Page 114 STAT. 464]] Public Law 106-229 106th Congress An Act To facilitate the use of electronic records and signatures in interstate or foreign commerce. <<NOTE: June 30, 2000 - [S. 761]>> Be it enacted by the Senate and House of Representatives of the United States of America in Congress <<NOTE: Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act. Contracts.>> assembled, SECTION 1. <<NOTE: 15 USC 7001 note.>> SHORT TITLE. This Act may be cited as the ``Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act''. TITLE I--ELECTRONIC RECORDS AND SIGNATURES IN COMMERCE SEC. 101. GENERAL RULE <<NOTE: 15 USC 7001.>> OF VALIDITY. (a) In General.--Notwithstanding any statute, regulation, or other rule of law (other than this title and title II), with respect to any transaction in or affecting interstate or foreign commerce-- (1) a signature, contract, or other record relating to such transaction may not be denied legal effect, validity, or enforceability solely because it is in electronic form; and (2) a contract relating to such transaction may not be denied legal effect, validity, or enforceability solely because an electronic signature or electronic record was used in its formation. (b) Preservation of Rights and Obligations.--This title does not-- (1) limit, alter, or otherwise affect any requirement imposed by a statute, regulation, or rule of law relating to the rights and obligations of persons under such statute, regulation, or rule of law other than a requirement that contracts or other records be written, signed, or in nonelectronic form; or (2) require any person to agree to use or accept electronic records or electronic signatures, other than a governmental agency with respect to a record other than a contract to which it is a party. (c) Consumer Disclosures.-- (1) Consent to electronic records.--Notwithstanding subsection (a), if a statute, regulation, or other rule of law requires that information relating to a transaction or transactions in or affecting interstate or foreign commerce be provided or made available to a consumer in writing, the use of an electronic record to provide or make available (whichever [[Page 114 STAT. 465]] is required) such information satisfies the requirement that such information be in writing if-- (A) the consumer has affirmatively consented to such use and has not withdrawn such consent; (B) the consumer, prior to consentin
perturbation.justification: The change in the contract, mandating *only* email as a form of notification, contradicts the need for verifiable receipt of important notices, especially concerning arbitration. The Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act (Snippet 1) states that electronic records and signatures cannot be denied legal effect solely because they are in electronic form. However, it also preserves requirements imposed by other laws regarding rights and obligations. The Law Explanation notes that arbitration rules or applicable law may require 'written notice' with proof of receipt. While the E-Sign act validates electronic signatures, it doesn't override specific requirements for ensuring notice is received, something that exclusive email use might not guarantee, thus potentially contradicting the intent of legally sound notification, and weakening required notification standards. The modified clause would likely fail legal scrutiny.
